title: Psalms 15
subtitle: Who May Dwell in Your Sanctuary?

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “Who May Live on Your Holy Mountain?”

heb.gloss: to sojourn / to dwell as guest
heb.paragraph: "LORD, who may dwell (gūr) in your sacred tent? Who may live on your holy mountain?" (v.1). The entrance question: who qualifies for God’s presence? The answer is ethical, not ritual: blameless conduct, righteous action, truthful speech, no slander, no wrong to neighbour.

panels.ctx: A Torah entrance liturgy — the worshipper asks at the gate: who may enter? The answer (vv.2–5): the one whose walk is blameless, who does right, speaks truth, does no wrong to a neighbour, keeps oaths even when it hurts, and does not accept bribes. Character, not credentials.

cross.note: "Who may ascend the mountain of the LORD?" — the companion entrance liturgy.
"Who can dwell with the consuming fire?" — the prophetic version.

notes.note: "Who may dwell in your sacred tent?" The question assumes not everyone qualifies. Access to God requires character.
"Whose walk is blameless, who does what is righteous, who speaks the truth from their heart." Three categories: conduct, action, speech. All internal → external.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the "sacred tent" and "holy mountain" are Zion’s temple. The question is both literal (who enters the sanctuary?) and theological (who lives in God’s presence?).
Calvin: the ethical requirements show that faith and morality are inseparable. Calvin: you cannot worship God on Sunday and wrong your neighbour on Monday.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: gūr means to sojourn as a guest. Dwelling with God is guest-status, not ownership. We are guests in God’s house, admitted by his grace and expected to honour his rules.
Kidner: the entrance question was asked at the temple gate. The priests answered with the ethical requirements. This psalm preserves that liturgy.
VanGemeren: the entrance requirements are entirely moral, not ceremonial. No mention of sacrifice, purity rituals, or priestly requirements. Character is the admission ticket.
Goldingay: "speaks the truth from their heart" — not just accurate speech but sincere speech. The heart and the tongue are aligned. No double heart (cf. Ps 12:2).

sections.header: Verses 4–5 — “The One Who Does These Things Will Never Be Shaken”

heb.gloss: will never be shaken / will not be moved
heb.paragraph: "Whoever does these things will never be shaken (lōʾ yimmōṭ)" (v.5). The psalm’s reward: stability. The person of integrity has a foundation that does not collapse. This connects to Psalm 1’s tree and Psalm 11’s foundations — the righteous are rooted, unshakable, permanent.

panels.ctx: More requirements: despise the vile, honour the God-fearing, keep oaths even at cost, lend without usury, refuse bribes against the innocent (vv.4–5a). The promise: "whoever does these things will never be shaken" (v.5b). The psalm is 5 verses of character and one sentence of reward. The emphasis is clear: the life matters; the stability follows.

cross.note: "The wise man built his house on the rock... it did not fall" — the same unshakable-foundation promise.
"The righteous will never be shaken" — the identical promise.

notes.note: "Who keeps an oath even when it hurts." Integrity means fulfilling commitments when the cost is high, not just when it is convenient.
"Whoever does these things will never be shaken." The one-sentence promise. Character produces stability.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "keeps an oath even to their own hurt" is the most demanding requirement. It distinguishes genuine integrity from convenient morality.

Calvin: "never shaken" is conditional on character, but the character itself is a gift of grace. Calvin: we do not earn stability; we receive the grace that produces the character that results in stability.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "swears to their own hurt" means fulfilling a vow even when circumstances change and the vow becomes costly. Integrity is tested in the gap between promise and fulfilment.
The NET: lōʾ yimmōṭ (not be moved/shaken) uses the mot root — the same root in Psalm 10:6. The righteous share the REAL stability the wicked only imagined.
Kidner: "never shaken" is the psalm’s payoff: not prosperity or fame but stability. The person of integrity stands when everything around them falls.
VanGemeren: the requirements escalate: keeping costly oaths, refusing profitable bribes. Integrity is tested by what you give up, not what you gain.
Goldingay: "never be shaken" echoes the wicked’s boast in 10:6 ("I will never be shaken"). The difference: the wicked’s confidence is self-generated; the righteous person’s is God-grounded.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVwhose tongue utters no slander, who does no wrong to a neighbor, and casts no slur on others;ESVwho does not slander with his tongue and does no evil to his neighbor, nor takes up a reproach against his friend;NIVThe one whose walk is blameless, who does what is righteous, who speaks the truth from their heart;ESVHe who walks blamelessly and does what is right and speaks truth in his heart;

rec.text: ""Who may dwell in your sacred tent?" The question assumes not everyone qualifies. Access to God requires character."

rows.label: The Promise — 15:4–5
rows.text: Costly oaths kept, bribes refused. "Whoever does these things will never be shaken."

hebtext.gloss: to sojourn / to dwell as guest

hebtext.gloss: will never be shaken / will not be moved
hebtext.note: "Whoever does these things will never be shaken (lōʾ yimmōṭ)" (v.5). The psalm’s reward: stability. The person of integrity has a foundation that does not collapse. This connects to Psalm 1’s tree and Psalm 11’s foundations — the righteous are rooted, unshakable, permanent.

themes.note: Holiness peaks: entrance requirements are ethical, not ritual. Worship: the temple-gate liturgy. Justice: costly integrity.
